ideaBrief.title: Risk Manager
ideaBrief.audience: University students in computer science and project management courses
ideaBrief.coreMetaphor: Managing a software project as a high-stakes adventure filled with risks and challenges
ideaBrief.successVision: Players develop practical risk management skills through immersive gameplay while enjoying a retro-themed simulation

state.projectTitle: Risk Manager: An IT Project Management Simulation Game
state.deliverableArea: IT Risk Management Simulation — Browser-Based Serious Game
state.problemStatement: Students lack hands-on experience in identifying, assessing, and mitigating project risks in software development
state.targetPopulation: Undergraduate and graduate students in computer science, software engineering, and project management programs
successCriteria: ≥15% improvement in pre/post quiz scores on risk response strategy and EMV calculation items
≥80% of students correctly match all four PMBOK risk response strategies to their definitions in the post-session quiz
≥20% improvement in Assignment 2 risk register completeness rubric score versus prior-semester control group
≥4.0/5.0 mean satisfaction score on L1 post-session survey measuring enjoyment and PM concept clarity
≥70% of students report at least one playthrough beyond the first, indicating replay and iterative learning behaviour
≥50% of students attempt a second playthrough within the 20-minute classroom session

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Students will identify the four primary PMBOK risk response strategies and recognise their standard definitions.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Reviewing the Risk Card interface where Mitigate, Avoid, Transfer, and Accept options are explicitly defined with their associated financial costs and expected effects.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Pre/post-session multiple-choice quiz requiring students to match each strategy name to its correct PMBOK definition and typical use case.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Students will explain the Expected Monetary Value formula and describe how probability and financial impact jointly determine risk priority.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Reading the live EMV chip on every Risk Card (Probability% × Impact$) and using it to compare competing risks before selecting a response.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Quiz item requiring students to compute EMV for a given risk scenario and rank two risks by priority using their calculated values.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Students will apply appropriate PMBOK risk response strategies to simulated scenarios based on budget, schedule, and probability constraints.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Actively managing the daily project simulation by selecting responses for triggered risk cards within a finite $100,000 budget and 30-day deadline.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Scenario-based quiz item requiring students to select the most mathematically optimal risk response given specific impact, probability, and remaining-budget data.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Students will analyse the interdependence between risk response investment decisions and downstream project budget, schedule, and morale outcomes.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Monitoring the Budget, Deadline, and Morale HUD counters across turns and observing how each risk response choice cascades into subsequent resource availability.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Post-session written reflection (200 words) where students deconstruct one specific risk response decision and trace its systemic effect on project outcome.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Students will evaluate competing risk response options under severe resource scarcity and justify their chosen strategy using EMV-based reasoning.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Resolving high-stakes risk events late in the simulation when budget is nearly exhausted, forcing explicit cost-benefit justification for each response selection.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Assignment 2 rubric evaluation: assessing the rationale provided in the risk register for each documented response strategy.

bloomOutcomes.outcome: Students will formulate a comprehensive, data-driven project risk register integrating probability, impact, EMV, and proactive PMBOK response strategies.
bloomOutcomes.activity: Synthesising gameplay experience — observed risk triggers, response costs, and outcome consequences — to anticipate and document risks in a structured register.
bloomOutcomes.assessment: Instructor evaluation of the final Project Charter submission, specifically scoring the completeness, probability/impact ratings, and response strategy rationale in the risk register.

state.narrativeTheme: RiskManager: IT Project Management Simulation Game situates the player as a Project Manager responsible for delivering PROJECT: NEON — a real software product under simultaneous budget, deadline, and team-morale constraints. The game's design is governed by five evidence-based frameworks. Bloom Taxonomy structures the cognitive arc: early turns engage the Remember and Understand levels (naming the four PMBOK response strategies, reading the EMV formula on risk cards), mid-game escalates to Apply and Analyze (computing optimal responses under budget pressure, balancing the Iron Triangle), and the Post-Mortem report targets Evaluate (judging one's own decisions against measurable project outcomes). Self-Determination Theory shapes all motivational mechanics: Autonomy is embodied in the player's unrestricted choice of risk response strategy and team composition; Competence grows through the live EMV indicator and the personalised Post-Mortem feedback paragraph; Relatedness is built through the named employee roster, synergy bonuses, and the shared team-morale resource. Mechanics-Dynamics-Aesthetics disciplines the design layer: the Day Advance mechanic, four PMBOK response buttons, employee overtime, and upgrade purchases (Mechanics) produce emergent behaviours of risk prioritisation, budget triage, and Burnout avoidance (Dynamics) that yield aesthetics of Challenge and Discovery. HEXAD informs player-type coverage: Achiever types target the Victory milestone and budget surplus; Explorer types discover hidden synergy pairs and conditional event chains; Philanthropist types engage the eight-card educational sidebar; Disruptor types probe all three Game Over conditions deliberately. Kirkpatrick four-level evaluation governs outcome measurement across the deployment lifecycle: Level 1 Reaction is captured by a post-session satisfaction survey; Level 2 Learning by a short PMBOK quiz testing EMV recall and strategy mapping; Level 3 Behaviour by instructor observation of risk vocabulary use in subsequent coursework assignments; Level 4 Results by cohort-level project delivery rates in end-of-term capstone modules.
state.gameOverview: Risk Manager is a 30-day simulation game where players manage a software development project called PROJECT: NEON. Players must hire and manage a team, handle daily risks and events, make strategic decisions through dilemmas, and deliver the project on time while maintaining budget and morale.
state.sdtAutonomy: Players have freedom to choose team composition, risk mitigation strategies, and daily management decisions
state.sdtCompetence: Success in risk mitigation and project delivery builds player competence through skill-based challenges
state.sdtRelatedness: Team synergy mechanics and morale systems create social connections between virtual team members

rewardStructure.elite: Players achieving a Victory Score of 80,000 or higher earn the Budget Master badge and classroom leaderboard recognition for exceptional risk management and resource efficiency.
rewardStructure.professional: Players scoring between 40,000 and 79,999 earn the standard completion certificate for successfully delivering the software project using a balanced PMBOK risk response strategy.
rewardStructure.access: Students who complete the 30-day simulation by reaching the 1,000 progress-point milestone — regardless of budget or morale depletion — earn the baseline learning outcome credit.

mechanics: Risk Cards: Daily random risk events that must be addressed
Employee Management: Hire, fire, and manage team members with different skills
Daily Progress System: Advance project through daily actions
Morale System: Maintain team motivation through various means
Budget Management: Allocate money for hires, upgrades, and risk mitigation
Dilemma Choices: Make tough decisions that affect project outcomes
Critical Success Events: Random positive events that boost progress
Bug Outbreaks: Special risk events that require immediate attention
Upgrade System: Purchase permanent improvements to team capabilities
Contingency Reserve: Emergency fund for critical risk payments

conceptMappings.mechanic: 4 Buttons on Risk Cards (Mitigate $2k, Avoid $5k, Transfer $3k, Accept $0)
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player allocates finite budget against statistical probability, forcing cost-benefit reasoning per risk
conceptMappings.rationale: Forces practical application of PMBOK Ch. 11 response planning; each button's cost models real-world consulting and insurance fees.

conceptMappings.concept: Expected Monetary Value — Quantitative Risk Analysis (PMBOK 11.4)
conceptMappings.mechanic: Live EMV chip on every Risk Card displaying Probability% × Impact$
conceptMappings.dynamic: Players compare EMV values across concurrent risks to triage which threats to address first with limited budget
conceptMappings.rationale: Operationalises PMBOK quantitative analysis; converts abstract probability theory into an actionable dollar figure visible during every decision.

conceptMappings.mechanic: 15 distinct risk event types spawning dynamically from Day 4 with probability, cost, delay, and morale fields
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player continuously encounters new threat categories requiring ongoing identification and assessment throughout the project lifecycle
conceptMappings.rationale: Demonstrates that risk identification is an iterative process — not a one-time setup — matching the PMBOK principle of continuous risk monitoring.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Risk Cards display High / Medium / Low severity level with colour-coded accent bars
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player must triage which risks are critical versus acceptable when budget cannot cover all responses simultaneously
conceptMappings.rationale: Replicates the Probability-Impact Matrix used in PMBOK qualitative analysis; forces prioritisation decisions identical to real project risk workshops.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Maintaining a budget buffer above $0 across 30 simulation days
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player learns not to exhaust the entire $100k budget early, preserving reserves for late-game high-impact risk triggers
conceptMappings.rationale: Teaches the necessity of contingency and management reserves against unknown-unknowns (PMBOK reserve analysis).

conceptMappings.mechanic: Daily event log recording all triggered risks, chosen responses, and financial outcomes
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player reviews the log each day to detect patterns — recurring risk types, morale decay trends — and adjusts mitigation investment accordingly
conceptMappings.rationale: Models the continuous risk monitoring process; the event log is the in-game equivalent of the Risk Register update cycle.

conceptMappings.concept: Proactive Risk Mitigation Investment (PMBOK 11.5 — Mitigate strategy)
conceptMappings.mechanic: Technology Upgrade Shop offering permanent probability-reduction purchases (Code Review, CI/CD Pipeline, Security Audit, Risk Monitoring Dashboard)
conceptMappings.dynamic: Players weigh one-time upgrade cost against cumulative risk-probability savings over remaining project days — a return-on-investment calculation
conceptMappings.rationale: Models real PM infrastructure investments that reduce residual risk probability; reinforces that Mitigate is a proactive, investment-based strategy.

conceptMappings.concept: Risk Burnout and Resource Risk (PMBOK 11.2 — Human Resource Risks)
conceptMappings.mechanic: Morale System (0–100%) with decay rate and productivity multiplier linkage
conceptMappings.dynamic: Neglecting team wellbeing mathematically reduces daily progress and increases bug event probability, cascading into schedule and budget overruns
conceptMappings.rationale: Demonstrates that team burnout is a quantified project risk — documented in PMBOK resource management risk categories — not merely a soft concern.

conceptMappings.mechanic: Scope Creep risk card triggering unplanned budget and schedule deductions when accepted without mitigation
conceptMappings.dynamic: Player must decide between costly avoidance/transfer responses versus accepting scope expansion that compounds future risk exposure
conceptMappings.rationale: Makes Scope Creep a tangible, measurable threat consistent with its PMBOK classification as a top cause of project failure.

mdaAnalysis.mechanics: Players interact with the Risk Card system to select PMBOK-aligned responses (Mitigate, Avoid, Transfer, Accept), manage a $100,000 budget, hire from 8 distinct specialist roles, and advance daily project cycles where progress is calculated using productivity, morale multipliers, and synergy bonuses. The Upgrade Shop allows permanent risk-probability reductions, and the Event Log records all decisions for continuous monitoring.
mdaAnalysis.dynamics: Aggressively accepting risks to save budget creates compounding failure states and cascading bug events later in the project, while heavy investment in risk mitigation and team morale generates high-velocity productivity loops — forcing players to constantly balance short-term budget conservation against long-term risk exposure. EMV-based comparison of concurrent risks drives prioritisation habits that mirror real PM practice.
mdaAnalysis.aesthetics: The simulation primarily evokes Challenge through tight budget and deadline constraints with no safe dominant strategy, while intense visual feedback (screen shakes, warning vignettes, budget flash animations) creates Sensation — making the abstract consequences of poor risk management emotionally impactful and educationally memorable.

scenarios.title: Total Risk Avoidance Strategy
scenarios.setup: Player is granted a $10,000 initial budget bonus but risk events spawn at double the normal rate from Day 4 onward, creating pressure to use expensive Avoid responses.
scenarios.winCondition: Complete the 30-day project with zero critical risk triggers and a budget greater than zero.
scenarios.loseCondition: Bankruptcy caused by overspending on Avoid ($5k) and Transfer ($3k) responses without sufficient contingency reserve.
scenarios.outcome: Demonstrates the unsustainable financial cost of attempting total risk avoidance — the PMBOK principle that Accept is rational for low-EMV risks.

scenarios.title: Aggressive Risk Acceptance Under Velocity Pressure
scenarios.setup: Project deadline is compressed to 20 days. Initial morale is high. Accepting all risks saves budget but accumulates residual risk exposure.
scenarios.winCondition: Reach the 1,000 progress-point milestone before Day 20 by maintaining team productivity without triggering catastrophic risk cascades.
scenarios.loseCondition: Compounding triggered risks deplete budget or destroy morale before the deadline. Compassion model applies: losePoints=0 to encourage replay and reflection.
scenarios.outcome: Shows the danger of over-relying on Accept responses: EMV-based analysis reveals the hidden long-term cost of ignoring residual risks.

scenarios.title: Optimal EMV-Balanced Risk Management
scenarios.setup: Standard Day 1 parameters. No single response strategy is mathematically dominant — players must compute and compare EMV values for each risk before responding.
scenarios.winCondition: Maximise the final Victory Score by completing the project with more than $30,000 remaining and team morale above 70%.
scenarios.loseCondition: Failing to reach 1,000 points before Day 30 due to unmitigated high-EMV risks. Compassion model applies to encourage iterative replay.
scenarios.outcome: Encourages mastery of PMBOK Ch. 11 risk allocation by rewarding balanced, data-driven EMV decision-making over any extreme strategy.

gamePhases.description: Player allocates initial budget, hires first team members, and encounters the first wave of risk cards — establishing the core risk identification and response loop.

gamePhases.phase: Phase 2 — Risk Analysis and Response Planning (Days 6–15)
gamePhases.description: New risk categories spawn, EMV comparisons become necessary, upgrade investments are evaluated, and the morale system introduces human-resource risk dynamics.
Compounding risk triggers test earlier response decisions; contingency reserve depletion forces triage; Critical Success events reward high-morale teams.
Final sprint toward the 1,000-point milestone; any unmitigated residual risks fire at maximum probability; Victory or Game Over triggers the Post-Mortem report.

userStories.want: view the financial cost, probability, and Expected Monetary Value of a specific risk
userStories.soThat: I can apply quantitative risk analysis before choosing a PMBOK response strategy
userStories.acc: Given the Risk Center modal is open, When I view a Risk Card, Then the system displays the risk's exact dollar impact, trigger probability percentage, and computed EMV value before I select any response.

userStories.want: hire specialist team members whose roles permanently reduce specific risk-category probabilities
userStories.soThat: I can apply the PMBOK Mitigate strategy proactively through workforce investment rather than reactive per-risk spending
userStories.acc: Given I have sufficient budget, When I hire the DevOps specialist, Then the probability of server-related risk triggers is permanently reduced by 25% for the remainder of the simulation.

userStories.want: see the financial and schedule impact of each risk response immediately after selecting it
userStories.soThat: I receive direct EMV-grounded feedback that reinforces which PMBOK response strategy produced the best outcome given the risk's probability and impact
userStories.acc: Given I select the Mitigate response on a risk card, When the action is confirmed, Then the budget counter decreases by $2,000 and the risk's probability is visibly reduced on subsequent appearances.

userStories.want: experience visual and immediate consequences when an unmitigated risk triggers
userStories.soThat: I emotionally understand the financial and schedule severity of neglecting project threats, reinforcing the value of proactive risk response planning
userStories.acc: Given a Critical-level risk was Accepted, When the probability roll triggers it, Then the budget counter visibly decreases by the full impact amount, the deadline counter decreases, and a warning visual effect plays.

userStories.want: receive a Post-Mortem report at the end of every session summarising my risk decisions and their outcomes
userStories.soThat: I can evaluate the quality of my own risk management strategy and identify which PMBOK response choices produced the best cost-benefit results
userStories.acc: Given the project simulation ends (Victory or Game Over), When the Post-Mortem modal opens, Then it displays total budget spent on each response strategy type, number of triggered risks by category, and a feedback paragraph referencing PMBOK concepts.

frs.desc: System shall simulate 30 in-game days with budget tracking from $100,000 initial balance, deducting daily employee salaries and risk response costs each turn

System shall display 15 risk event types as interactive Risk Cards showing probability %, financial impact $, EMV $, schedule delay in days, and morale damage — with Mitigate, Avoid, Transfer, and Accept response buttons
System shall allow hiring of 8 specialist employee roles, each deducting a daily salary from budget and applying a permanent probability reduction to their associated risk category
System shall calculate daily project progress from employee productivity multiplied by morale multiplier and synergy bonus, advancing the progress bar toward the 1,000-point victory threshold
System shall trigger random risk events each day by rolling against each active risk's current probability value, reflecting the PMBOK principle of continuous risk monitoring
System shall apply visual feedback effects (budget flash, warning vignette, screen shake) when a risk triggers and causes financial or schedule damage, making consequences immediately perceptible

System shall provide a Technology Upgrade Shop with permanent risk-probability-reduction purchases (Code Review −20% bug risk, CI/CD Pipeline −25% integration risk, Security Audit −30% vulnerability risk, Risk Monitoring Dashboard −15% all risks)

System shall display a Post-Mortem Report on session end showing: completion percentage, remaining budget (Cost Variance), remaining deadline (Schedule Variance), final morale, risk response strategy distribution, and an AI-generated feedback paragraph referencing PMBOK terminology

System shall display a Victory screen when progress reaches 1,000 points and a Game Over screen when budget, deadline, or morale reaches zero, with a Victory Score calculation rewarding efficient risk management

System shall maintain a persistent event log recording all triggered risks, chosen responses, costs incurred, and outcomes for the player to review at any point during the session via the Risk Center modal

nfrs.desc: Game session shall complete in 15–20 minutes for a single playthrough, fitting within a standard university lab period alongside introduction and debrief time
Application shall run entirely in-browser with no server-side dependencies, deployable as a static bundle on any CDN or university web server
All UI text shall be rendered as DOM elements to ensure screen reader accessibility for students with visual impairments
Application shall load within 3 seconds on a standard university network connection using the Vite production build
Codebase shall be structured as a Vue 3 single-page application using Composition API, with risk probability values extracted to a configuration object for balance tuning without code changes
Image rendering shall use pixelated CSS interpolation for consistent pixel art aesthetic matching the retro PM simulation narrative theme

sessionSteps.name: Onboarding and PMBOK Theory Primer
sessionSteps.description: Facilitator introduces the simulation, connects it to PMBOK Ch. 11 Risk Management Planning (risk identification, qualitative/quantitative analysis, response strategies), and students complete the conceptual pre-quiz.

sessionSteps.name: Risk Identification and Team Setup
sessionSteps.description: Players allocate their initial $100k budget, hire specialist team members whose roles reduce specific risk-category probabilities, and encounter the first wave of dynamically spawned risk cards.

sessionSteps.name: Risk Monitoring and Response Decision Loop
sessionSteps.description: Students manage compounding risk triggers, apply EMV-based triage to prioritise which risks to Mitigate, Avoid, Transfer, or Accept, and attempt to maintain team morale as the 30-day deadline approaches.

sessionSteps.name: Post-Mortem Review and Assessment
sessionSteps.description: The facilitator leads a classroom retrospective to compare Victory Scores and risk response strategy distributions, followed by administration of the post-session quiz and L1 satisfaction survey.

kpis.method: 10-item Likert satisfaction survey administered via Google Forms immediately after the session
kpis.target: ≥4.0 / 5.0 mean score on overall enjoyment, engagement, and perceived clarity of PMBOK risk management concepts

kpis.metric: Learning — Risk Response and EMV Comprehension
kpis.method: 15-item conceptual pre/post quiz administered 48 hours before and 48 hours after the simulation via Google Forms
kpis.target: ≥15% mean score improvement on PMBOK risk response strategy application; ≥80% accuracy on EMV calculation items

kpis.method: Instructor evaluation of students' subsequent Project Charter submissions (Assignment 2) using a standardised risk register completeness rubric
kpis.target: ≥20% improvement in Assignment 2 risk register completeness rubric score versus prior-semester control group

kpis.method: End-of-semester evaluation of capstone project risk documentation quality, comparing simulation cohorts against historical cohort data
kpis.target: Statistically significant improvement in capstone project risk register completeness and response strategy justification grades

state.logPrivacy: All gameplay data is anonymized and aggregated for educational research purposes only
state.dataCollectionPlan: Game logs player decisions, risk outcomes, and completion metrics for analysis of learning effectiveness

tuslerRisks.risk: Server Crash — database overloads under project pressure
tuslerRisks.preventive: Hire the DevOps specialist early to apply a permanent −25% server risk probability reduction; purchase the Cloud Scaling upgrade by Day 10.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Select Mitigate immediately to halve the trigger probability; if budget is critical, Transfer the financial impact via the insurance response option.

tuslerRisks.risk: Scope Creep — client requests unplanned features mid-project
tuslerRisks.preventive: Hire the Project Manager role to enforce scope boundaries and reduce Scope Creep probability by 30%; document original scope in the risk register.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Transfer the risk by negotiating client-paid buffer; Avoid entirely (remove from pool) if budget permits and timeline is critical.

tuslerRisks.risk: Critical Bug — show-stopping defect in core functionality
tuslerRisks.preventive: Hire the QA Specialist to reduce bug probability by 25%; purchase the Code Review upgrade for an additional −20% reduction.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Mitigate aggressively — an unmitigated Critical Bug triggers both a budget deduction and a morale penalty, compounding future risk.

tuslerRisks.risk: Security Vulnerability — critical zero-day discovered in codebase
tuslerRisks.preventive: Hire the Security Expert to reduce vulnerability probability by 30%; purchase the Security Audit upgrade for ongoing protection.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Transfer the financial impact through cyber insurance or third-party indemnification; Accept only if EMV is below current budget threshold.

tuslerRisks.risk: Team Burnout — overworked members lose productivity
tuslerRisks.preventive: Maintain morale above 70% by avoiding consecutive overtime assignments; purchase the Espresso Machine upgrade for passive morale recovery.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Accept only when morale is high enough to absorb the penalty; otherwise Mitigate by reducing overtime and scheduling a rest day.

tuslerRisks.risk: Technical Debt — accumulated shortcuts surface as cascading failures
tuslerRisks.preventive: Purchase the Code Review and CI/CD Pipeline upgrades early; invest in QA hiring to prevent debt accumulation from the start.
tuslerRisks.responsive: Mitigate immediately — Technical Debt left unaddressed increases bug probability for all subsequent days, creating a compounding risk cascade.

supportTeam.name: Yusuf Altunel
supportTeam.resp: Deploy the game URL to the lab environment, introduce the PMBOK risk management context, facilitate the post-game debrief, and administer pre/post quizzes.

supportTeam.name: TBA
supportTeam.resp: Assist students with browser access, collect L1 satisfaction surveys, manage classroom leaderboard tally, and support students encountering UI issues.

supportTeam.name: University IT Department
supportTeam.resp: Ensure lab computers have unrestricted browser access to the Netlify or Vercel static hosting domain and that no firewall rules block the application assets.

state.facilitatorRole: Introduce the game mechanics, observe student decision-making, facilitate post-game discussion on risk management strategies, and connect gameplay to real-world project management concepts
state.inScope:
30-day project simulation with budget and deadline constraints
15 distinct risk event types with probability, cost, EMV, and schedule impact
8 hireable specialist roles each providing permanent risk-probability reductions
6 technology upgrade options modifying risk probabilities
Morale system (0–100%) affecting productivity multiplier and critical success probability
Synergy system rewarding compatible specialist role pair hiring
Particle effects, screen shake, and vignette danger overlay on risk triggers
Day Summary modal with sprint-day progress and triggered event data
Victory score calculation and three Game Over conditions (budget, deadline, morale)
Post-Mortem report with PMBOK Lessons Learned structure and AI feedback
state.outScope:
Multiplayer or collaborative play modes
Persistent player accounts or cross-session leaderboards
Network-dependent backend or database infrastructure
Mobile-native application (browser-only in current version)
Audio and sound effects
Agile or Scrum methodology-specific mechanics unrelated to PMBOK risk planning
Stakeholder communication simulations outside risk response context
WBS decomposition or scope management educational content
state.whyMotivation: Students consistently underperform on risk management assignments because they engage with risk as an abstract checklist. The game creates emotional memory of risk consequences through visceral visual feedback — red vignette, screen shake, budget counter flash — which is more pedagogically durable than passive lecture. Research on game-based learning demonstrates significantly higher retention of procedural knowledge such as risk decision-making when compared to traditional instruction alone (Mayer 2019; Plass et al. 2020).
state.howEthics: The simulation employs a strict compassion model: no personal data is stored, and failure states are purely instructional with losePoints=0 to maintain a zero-penalty learning environment. All gameplay data is anonymised by student number, and findings are used exclusively for educational research and simulation improvement. Students participate voluntarily and face no academic penalty for in-game decisions.
